title: TSMC's Moat Revealed: 93,000 Certified IPs Build a Hundred-Billion-Dollar Ecosystem Barrier
gist: SemiAnalysis argues TSMC's true moat is not process technology or EUV equipment but its EDA/IP ecosystem — certified IP on its Open Innovation Platform grew from 3,000 items in 2010 to 93,000 last year — which, combined with $50-100 million re-spin costs, makes customers 'unwilling to migrate, and afraid to migrate,' meaning Samsung and Intel face an ecosystem war, not just a process race.

themes.theme: TSMC's true moat: the EDA/IP ecosystem

insights.claim: SemiAnalysis argues that TSMC's most difficult-to-replicate moat is not its advanced process technology, EUV lithography equipment, or manufacturing yields, but the EDA and silicon IP ecosystem it has spent over a decade building around its fabs.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Reframes the competitive analysis of foundry investing: process-node comparisons (Samsung, Intel catching up on PPA) may understate TSMC's durable advantage, which lives in ecosystem lock-in rather than transistor specs.
insights.quote: the most difficult-to-replicate moat for the world's leading foundry, TSMC, is not its widely recognized advanced process technology, extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography equipment, or industry-leading manufacturing yields. Rather, it is the electronic design automation (EDA) and silicon intellectual property (IP) ecosystem
insights.locator: Semiconductor research firm SemiAnalysis recently dropped

insights.claim: TSMC's certified silicon IP library on its Open Innovation Platform surged from approximately 3,000 items in 2010 to 93,000 items last year, a more than 31-fold increase over 15 years, covering SerDes, HBM, PCIe, UCIe, memory interfaces, and chiplet interconnects.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A quantified, trackable metric (93,000 certified IPs) that investors can use as a proxy for TSMC's ecosystem lead over Samsung Foundry and Intel Foundry, which lack comparable IP density.
insights.quote: TSMC's certified silicon IP library has surged from approximately 3,000 items in 2010 to 93,000 items last year—a more than 31-fold increase over 15 years.

insights.claim: SemiAnalysis describes a self-reinforcing feedback loop: ecosystem stickiness drives EDA profitability, which attracts more design projects, which further solidifies TSMC's market position.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A flywheel model investors can use to judge whether TSMC's lead is likely to compound rather than erode, implying durable pricing power and market share even absent a pure process edge.
insights.quote: "Ecosystem stickiness drives EDA profitability, which in turn attracts more design projects, ultimately solidifying TSMC's market position."

insights.claim: A single chip re-spin at an advanced node typically costs $50 million to $100 million and can delay time-to-market by 6 to 12 months, making customers prioritize reducing design risk over chasing a few percentage points of PPA improvement.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Explains the economic mechanism behind foundry switching costs — a concrete dollar figure ($50-100M) that investors can use to model the risk premium customers pay to stay with TSMC rather than qualify a cheaper alternative.
insights.quote: The cost of a single re-spin at an advanced node typically ranges from $50 million to $100 million, and can delay product time-to-market by 6 to 12 months.
insights.locator: What Locks Customers In Is Not Performance

insights.claim: Modern chip design flows (RTL synthesis, place-and-route, sign-off, physical verification) form a highly coupled tool chain where any change to a core EDA tool can force all subsequent verification to be redone; SemiAnalysis states bluntly, 'The entire flow itself is the lock-in.'
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Identifies the toolchain itself, not any single vendor relationship, as the true lock-in mechanism — implying that Samsung/Intel foundry-switching campaigns must replicate an entire coupled software stack, not just win a price negotiation.
insights.quote: SemiAnalysis states bluntly: "The entire flow itself is the lock-in."

insights.claim: IP modules like SerDes and HBM certified by TSMC are deeply tied to its process design kits (PDKs), meaning migrating a flagship ASIC to another foundry requires rebuilding the entire EDA tool chain and re-verifying a vast number of IPs — the fundamental reason catching up is harder than the market expects for Samsung Foundry and Intel Foundry.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Directly implies that Samsung and Intel's foundry turnaround narratives should be discounted for ecosystem-migration friction, not just judged on process-node timelines — relevant to positioning in INTC and Samsung Electronics.
insights.quote: If a customer wishes to migrate a flagship application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) to another foundry, it means rebuilding the EDA tool chain and re-verifying a vast number of IPs.
insights.locator: Furthermore, IP modules such as SerDes and HBM

insights.claim: The global EDA and IP market was worth approximately $18 billion last year and is projected to expand to $28 billion to $30 billion by 2030.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A concrete TAM growth figure (roughly 60-65% expansion by 2030) investors can use to size the EDA opportunity for Synopsys, Cadence, and Siemens.
insights.quote: the global EDA and IP market was worth approximately $18 billion last year, and is projected to expand to $28 billion to $30 billion by 2030

insights.claim: Synopsys (including Ansys), Cadence, and Siemens EDA collectively command over 85% of the EDA market, with FY2025 revenue estimates of roughly $8 billion for Synopsys, $5.3 billion for Cadence, and $2.2-2.5 billion for Siemens EDA.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Quantifies the EDA oligopoly structure and each vendor's revenue scale, a direct input for relative-value comparisons among Synopsys, Cadence, and Siemens in the EDA space.
insights.quote: the three giants—Synopsys (including its acquisition of Ansys), Cadence, and Siemens EDA—collectively command over 85% market share. Based on fiscal 2025 revenue estimates, Synopsys generates roughly $8 billion, Cadence approximately $5.3 billion, and Siemens EDA around $2.2 billion to $2.5 billion.
insights.locator: Among the players, the three giants

insights.claim: The EDA industry has posted a roughly 13% CAGR over the past decade, significantly outpacing semiconductor R&D investment growth, with the gap widening further since 2018 due to AI chip development, rising verification complexity at advanced nodes, and growing hardware emulation demand.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A 13% CAGR outpacing semiconductor R&D spend is a structural growth thesis for EDA stocks independent of semiconductor cyclicality, since design complexity itself is the growth driver.
insights.quote: Over the past decade, the EDA industry has posted a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of approximately 13%, significantly outpacing the growth rate of semiconductor R&D investment during the same period.
insights.locator: Over the past decade, the EDA industry

insights.claim: Synopsys CEO Sassine Ghazi has noted that AI-driven design complexity is pushing semiconductor R&D investment as a percentage of sales from roughly 6% toward 9%, allowing EDA vendors to benefit from both expanding R&D budgets and enhanced pricing power at advanced nodes.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A named executive's specific R&D-intensity trend (6% to 9% of sales) is a quantifiable tailwind for EDA vendor revenue growth and margin expansion.
insights.quote: Synopsys CEO Sassine Ghazi has noted that the design complexity brought by AI is pushing semiconductor R&D investment as a percentage of sales from roughly 6% toward 9%.
insights.locator: Synopsys CEO Sassine Ghazi has noted

insights.claim: Intel Foundry shifted its external customer focus from the 18A process to 18A-P and subsequent nodes, causing some IP originally developed around 18A to be delayed in commercialization and dragging down the IP revenue of related EDA vendors.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A concrete example showing that foundry roadmap changes propagate financial impact through the EDA/IP ecosystem, meaning Intel Foundry execution risk has spillover effects on EDA vendor revenue, not just Intel itself.
insights.quote: SemiAnalysis cites Intel Foundry as an example, noting that the company shifted its external customer focus from the 18A process to 18A-P and subsequent nodes. This caused some IP originally developed around 18A to be delayed in commercialization, dragging down the IP revenue of related EDA vendors
insights.locator: The Butterfly Effect of Intel's Roadmap Adjustment

insights.claim: SemiAnalysis concludes that Samsung and Intel face an 'ecosystem war,' not merely process competition — customers will not switch fabs just because a competitor claims superior PPA, since willingness to migrate depends on the complete portability of design tools and IP assets.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A high-conviction summary thesis: bet against near-term foundry-share shifts away from TSMC even if Samsung/Intel hit process parity, because ecosystem migration costs dominate the customer decision.
insights.quote: What Samsung and Intel face is not merely process competition, but an ecosystem war... This ecosystem that makes customers "unwilling to migrate, and afraid to migrate," is TSMC's most difficult-to-replicate moat
